speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really off this week—fatigue, chills, and some shortness of breath.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with your vitals and a quick history. Are you experiencing any chest discomfort or palpitations?

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure today is 106 mmHg—elevated, especially alongside your reported symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: Yes—I’ve had chest tightness since yesterday morning, especially when climbing stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level came back at 174 g/L—within normal range for an adult male, but let’s keep that in context with your other findings.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also been unusually tired, even after sleeping eight hours. My skin feels dry, and I’ve gained about four pounds in two weeks.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your symptoms, lab results, and exam, the primary diagnosis is Hypothyroidism—we’ll confirm with TSH and free T4 shortly.

speaker: patient
text: Hypothyroidism? That makes sense—I read about fatigue and weight gain, but I didn’t expect the chest tightness.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is elevated at 118 bpm—tachycardic, likely reactive to both thyroid dysfunction and possible underlying inflammation.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is 38.7 °C—fever is present, suggesting an acute component, possibly subacute thyroiditis given your presentation.

speaker: patient
text: I did have a sore throat last week—could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly. Subacute thyroiditis often follows a viral illness. We’ll check ESR and thyroid antibodies. In the meantime, let’s manage your symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: Okay. My chest tightness hasn’t improved with rest, and I feel lightheaded when standing up quickly.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature has come down to 37.9 °C—still low-grade, but trending downward, which is encouraging.

speaker: patient
text: That’s good. My energy level feels slightly better this afternoon, though I’m still fatigued.

speaker: doctor
text: Your repeat hemoglobin is now 180 g/L—mildly elevated from the earlier 174 g/L, likely reflecting hemoconcentration due to mild dehydration and fever.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll start levothyroxine at 25 mcg daily, and add ibuprofen for pain and inflammation. Avoid iodine-rich foods for now.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. Should I monitor my pulse or BP at home?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—track your heart rate and temperature twice daily. If chest tightness worsens or you develop swelling or voice changes, call immediately.

speaker: patient
text: Understood. And just to clarify—that first hemoglobin reading, 174 g/L, was consistent with what you saw earlier today.
